elements.sent-2411-001: The determination of molecular orientations is crucial for the three-dimensional reconstruction of Cryo-EM images.
elements.sent-2411-002: Traditionally addressed using the common-line method, this challenge is reformulated as a self-consistency error minimization problem constrained to rotation groups.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we consider the least-squared deviation (LUD) formulation and employ a Riemannian subgradient method to effectively solve the orientation determination problem.
elements.sent-2411-004: To enhance computational efficiency, a block stochastic version of the method is proposed, and its convergence properties are rigorously established.
elements.sent-2411-005: Extensive numerical evaluations reveal that our method not only achieves accuracy comparable to that of state-of-the-art methods but also delivers an average 20-fold speedup.
elements.sent-2411-006: Additionally, we implement a modified formulation and algorithm specifically designed to address scenarios characterized by very low SNR.
elements.sent-2411-007: Cryogenic electron microscopy (Cryo-EM) is crucial for reconstructing the 3D structures of macromolecules, providing evidence for the architecture of biomolecules \citep{frank2001cryo .}
elements.sent-2411-008: In Cryo-EM, identical biomolecules are rapidly frozen in their native states within vitreous ice, allowing for the generation of images from various orientations that are integrated to resolve the structure of the observed particles \citep{weissenberger2021understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-009: In the imaging process, only a small amount of electrons is used to reduce damage to molecules, resulting in images with a low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR).
elements.sent-2411-010: A prerequisite for 3D reconstruction is to obtain the projection direction of each image.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, this task is challenging because Cryo-EM projection directions are random and unknown \cite{bendory2020single, lyumkis2019challenges .}
elements.sent-2411-012: This randomness arises from the unpredictable orientations and positions of biomolecules during sample preparation and the randomizing effect of the electron beam during imaging.
elements.sent-2411-013: Furthermore, the high-energy electron beam can damage the sample, preventing repeated imaging from known directions.
elements.sent-2411-014: These challenges necessitate sophisticated computational methods to determine directions from randomly oriented noisy images.
elements.sent-2411-015: In Cryo-EM orientation determination, two streams of methods are used: the random-conical tilt technique \citep{radermacher1986new and common-line-based algorithms \citep{singer2010detecting,van1987angular,singer2011three,wang2013orientation .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: The random-conical tilt method collects images from particles at known and zero tilt angles, directly revealing particle orientations relative to the tilt axis.
elements.sent-2411-017: In contrast, common-line-based algorithms apply the Fourier projection slice theorem \citep{gustafsson1996mathematics , which posits that the 2D Fourier transform of a projection image forms a slice through the 3D Fourier space orthogonal to the projection direction.}
elements.sent-2411-018: This principle shows that two such transformed images intersect along a line—the common line—allowing for the inference of relative orientations by analyzing the similarity of Fourier coefficients along these intersections.
elements.sent-2411-019: The common-line approach is preferred as it uses the Fourier transform's properties to deduce spatial relationships without physically tilting the samples, thereby reducing radiation risk and avoiding sample damage.
elements.sent-2411-020: It also aids in 3D structural reconstruction by aligning multiple 2D images through their Fourier space intersections \cite{crowther1970three,rosenthal2015high .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Therefore, this paper focuses on the common-line-based approach.. Related work.
elements.sent-2411-022: In the field of common-line-based orientation determination algorithms, \cite{singer2011three derive least squares (LS) formulation on the rotation group, and address it with eigenvector relaxation and semi-definite programming (SDP) techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-023: To handle outliers from misdetected common-lines, \cite{wang2013orientation propose a robust least unsquared deviation (LUD) formulation, solved using semidefinite relaxation technique, and apply a spectral norm constraint to manage solution clustering under high noise.}
elements.sent-2411-024: These approaches, however, focus on relaxed versions of the problem.
elements.sent-2411-025: Furthermore, SDP is notoriously difficult to optimize for large-scale data.
elements.sent-2411-026: Recently, \cite{pan2023orientation solves the LUD formulation directly by transforming the LUD problem into a series of weighted LS problems, tackled through iterative reweighted least squares (IRLS) and projection gradient descent (PGD).. Motivations.}
elements.sent-2411-027: However, most existing methods are computationally inefficient, especially when millions of images are involved in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-028: Additionally, these methods struggle to maintain high accuracy as SNR decreases.
elements.sent-2411-029: These challenges persist as open problems in the field of orientation determination, motivating us to design more efficient algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-030: Notably, the LUD formulation, as described by \eqref{formula: LUD , presents a nonconvex and nonsmooth optimization problem on the Riemannian manifold.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Extensive research has been conducted on such problems, including works by \citep{absil2008optimization, yang2014optimality,li2021weakly, huang2022riemannian, chen2020proximal .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Specifically, \cite{liu2023resync has demonstrated the effectiveness of a Riemannian subgradient-based algorithm (ReSync) for robust least-unsquared rotation synchronization, achieving linear convergence.}
elements.sent-2411-033: This suggests potential for efficient problem-solving that could be further enhanced by integrating stochastic techniques.. Main contributions.
elements.sent-2411-034: In this paper, we build on the Riemannian subgradient-based algorithm (ReSync) framework introduced by \cite{liu2023resync , proposing a block stochastic Riemannian subgradient (BSGD) method to address the LUD formulation of the orientation determination problem.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Our contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-036: We propose a block stochastic Riemannian subgradient (BSGD) method to solve the LUD formulation of the orientation determination problem and establish its convergence property.
elements.sent-2411-037: Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves comparable accuracy and an averaging 20-fold speedup compared to existing common-line-based state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-038: To tackle the scenarios with very low SNR, we adopt the LUD formulation with a spectral norm constraint and modify our method to effectively solve it.
elements.sent-2411-039: Now we present a mathematical formulation of the orientation determination problem.
elements.sent-2411-040: Suppose we have collected $K$ projection images $P_1,...,P_K$ of the same molecule, each of them is taken at randomly unknown directions characterized by some rotation matrices $R_1,...,R_K \in \text{SO (3)$.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Our goal is to recover all the rotation matrices given these noisy projections.
elements.sent-2411-042: In this paper, we adopt a common-line-based approach to solve this problem.
elements.sent-2411-043: Firstly, 2D Fourier transformation is applied to projection images \cite{dutt1993fast,fessler2003nonuniform and pairs of common-lines are subsequently detected.}
elements.sent-2411-044: For readers interested in the detailed background of common-line method, refer to \cite{wang2013orientation .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Denote \(c_{ij ,c_{ji \in \mathbb{R ^3\) as the detected common lines in transformed projections $\hat P_i,\hat P_j$, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Since $\hat P_i,\hat P_j$ are inherently obtained by rotations $R_i,R_j$, by Fourier projection-slice theorem, the common-lines would remain to intersect at 3D Fourier space after rotated by $R_i,R_j$.
elements.sent-2411-047: Mathematically, such a relationship can be captured by \begin{equation}\label{eq:cl} R_i c_{ij} = R_j c_{ji}, \quad \forall~ 1\leq i < j \leq K.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eq:cl} R_i c_{ij} = R_j c_{ji}, \quad \forall~ 1\leq i < j \leq K.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-048: So far we have established the basic ideas of common-line-based approaches, then we consider formulating the orientation determination problem as an optimization problem on Stiefel manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-049: Specifically, we present two kinds of formulations, least square (LS) formulation and least unsquared deviation (LUD) formulation.. LS formulation.
elements.sent-2411-050: The overdetermined linear system \eqref{eq:cl is typically solved by the least square approach, which is formulated by the following manifold optimization problem \begin{equation}\label{formula: LS} \min_{R_1,...,R_K \in SO(3)} \sum_{i,j\in [K]} \|R_i c_{ij}-R_j c_{ji}\|_2^2.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Problem \eqref{formula: LS is a nonconvex optimization problem on the Riemannian manifold, favored for its smoothness properties.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Previous efforts include \cite{singer2011three addressing this formulation through eigenvector and semi-definite relaxations.}
elements.sent-2411-053: However, the least squares formulation lacks robustness as the sum of squared errors may be heavily influenced by outliers.. LUD formulation.
elements.sent-2411-054: A more robust alternative is the least unsquared deviation formulation, which aims to minimize the sum of unsquared errors: \begin{equation}\label{formula: LUD} \min_{R_1,...,R_K \in SO(3)} f(R)=\sum_{i,j\in [K]} \|R_i c_{ij}-R_j c_{ji}\|_2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-055: Problem \eqref{formula: LUD is nonconvex and nonsmooth, and is more difficult to optimize than the LS formulation.}
elements.sent-2411-056: We adopt the eigenvector relaxation method for efficient and high-quality initialization.
elements.sent-2411-057: Considering the LS formulation \eqref{formula: LS , it is equivalent to the maximization problem of the sum of dot products: \begin{equation}\label{init: dot} \max_{R_1,...,R_K \in SO(3)} \sum_{i,j\in [K]} R_i c_{ij} \cdot R_j c_{ji}=\sum_{i,j\in [K]} tr(R_i c_{ij}c_{ji}^T R_j^T)=tr(R^T M R)\end{equation} where $M_{ij =c_{ij c_{ji ^T\in \mathbb{R ^{3\times 3}$, and $M=[M_{ij ]\in\mathbb{R ^{3K\times 3K}$.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\label{init: dot} \max_{R_1,...,R_K \in SO(3)} \sum_{i,j\in [K]} R_i c_{ij} \cdot R_j c_{ji}=\sum_{i,j\in [K]} tr(R_i c_{ij}c_{ji}^T R_j^T)=tr(R^T M R)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-058: To make this optimization problem tractable, we relax the manifold constraints and consider the following problem: \begin{equation}\label{init: relaxed}  \max_{R\in R^{3K \times 3}} \quad tr(R^T M R) \quad subject to \quad R^T R = K\cdot I_3. \end{equation} The solution to the maximization problem \eqref{init: relaxed is therefore given by the top three eigenvectors $v_1, v_2, v_3$ (multiplied by constant $\sqrt{K}$) of $M$.}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{init: relaxed}  \max_{R\in R^{3K \times 3}} \quad tr(R^T M R) \quad subject to \quad R^T R = K\cdot I_3. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-059: Let $A=[v_1, v_2, v_3]\in \mathbb{R ^{3K\times 3}$ and it is then divided into $3\times 3$ matrices $A_1,...,A_K$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Subsequently, rotations are recovered by letting $R_i=U_i V_i^T$, where $A_i = U_i \Sigma V_i^T$ is its singular value decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-061: We consider applying the Riemannian subgradient method to solve problem \eqref{formula: LUD .}
elements.sent-2411-062: The main idea is to compute the subgradient in Euclidean space, then the Riemannian subgradient is derived from the projection on tangent space, and finally, the retraction operator is posed to guarantee feasibility.
elements.sent-2411-063: The pseudocode is presented by Algorithm \ref{algo:ReSync .}
elements.sent-2411-064: Here we start by deriving the form of Euclidean subdifferential: \begin{equation}\label{eq:full-subgrad} \partial f(R_i)= \sum_{j\in [K]} \partial f_{ij}(R_i)= \begin{dcases*} \sum_{j\in [K]} \frac{(R_i c_{ij}-R_j c_{ji})c_{ij}^T}{\|R_i c_{ij}-R_j c_{ji}\|} if $\|R_i c_{ij}-R_j c_{ji}\| \neq 0$ V\in R^{3\times 3}, \|V\|_F\leq 1 otherwise \end{dcases*}\end{equation} where $f_{ij =\|R_i c_{ij -R_j c_{ji}\|_2$.}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{eq:full-subgrad} \partial f(R_i)= \sum_{j\in [K]} \partial f_{ij}(R_i)= \begin{dcases*} \sum_{j\in [K]} \frac{(R_i c_{ij}-R_j c_{ji})c_{ij}^T}{\|R_i c_{ij}-R_j c_{ji}\|} if $\|R_i c_{ij}-R_j c_{ji}\| \neq 0$ V\in R^{3\times 3}, \|V\|_F\leq 1 otherwise \end{dcases*}\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-066: Subsequently, the Riemannian subgradient descent is given by $R_i^+=R_i-\mu \tilde \nabla_{\mathcal{R f(R_i)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Unfortunately, $R_i^+$ may violate the manifold constraint.
elements.sent-2411-068: One common approach is to employ a retraction operator to guarantee feasibility.
elements.sent-2411-069: For $SO(3)$, we can use a QR decomposition-based retraction and implement the Riemannian subgradient step as \begin{equation}\label{eq:retr} R_i^+=Retr_{R_i} (-\mu \tilde \nabla_{R} f(R_i))=Qr(R_i-\mu \tilde \nabla_{R} f(R_i)),\end{equation} where $\text{Qr (B)$ refers to the Q-factor in the thin QR decomposition of $B$.. \begin{minipage {0.46\textwidth.}}}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H]\label{alg:stochastic} \SetKwInput{KwInput}{Input} \SetKwInput{KwOutput}{Output} \SetKwInput{KwInitialize}{Initialize} \SetKwInput{KwRequire}{Require} \SetAlgoLined \DontPrintSemicolon \caption{Stochastic ReSync} \KwInput{Initialization \(R^0\), stepsize \(\mu_0\), filter ratio \(\rho_1, \rho_2\)} Set iteration count \( t = 0 \)\; \While{stopping criterion not met}{ Update the step size \( \mu_t \)\; Draw \(\mathcal{S},\mathcal{D}\subset [K]\) by \(\rho_1, \rho_2\) \; \For{\( i \in \mathcal{D} \)}{ \(\partial f(R_i)=\sum_{j \in \mathcal{S}} \partial f_{i,j}(R_i)\)\; execute line 6-7 in Algorithm \ref{algo:ReSync}\; } Update iteration count \( t = t + 1 \);\; }
elements.sent-2411-070: In this section, we explore stochastic variants of the ReSync algorithm to enhance its computational speed.
elements.sent-2411-071: We introduce a stochastic update approach where, instead of updating all rotation components using all common lines, we update only a subset of rotations, $\mathcal{D \subseteq [K], |D|=\rho_1 K$, using a selected subset of data, $\mathcal{S \subseteq [K], |S|=\rho_2 K$.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Specifically, the update rule \eqref{eq:full-subgrad is substituted by \begin{equation}\label{eq:BSGD-subgrad} \partial f(R_i)=\sum_{j \in S} \partial f_{i,j}(R_i), \quad \forall i\in D.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-073: We remark that many stochastic algorithms can be reduced to \eqref{eq:BSGD-subgrad , like stochastic gradient descent (SGD) \cite{amari1993backpropagation , block coordinate descent (BCD) \cite{tseng2001convergence , and block stochastic gradient descent (BSGD) \cite{xu2015block, zhao2014accelerated .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: ReSync-SGD: \(\rho_1=1, \rho_2=\rho\), all rotations are updated using $\rho$-fraction of data.
elements.sent-2411-075: ReSync-BCD: \(\rho_1=\rho, \rho_2=1\), $\rho$-fraction of rotations is updated by full subdifferential.
elements.sent-2411-076: ReSync-BSGD: \(\rho_1=\rho_2=\rho\) and \(\mathcal{S =\mathcal{D}\), only $\rho$-fraction of rotations is updated using the same fraction of data.}
elements.sent-2411-077: We refer to the parameter $\rho$ as the filter ratio, which dictates the proportion of data utilized in these updates.
elements.sent-2411-078: After computing the Euclidean subgradient, we apply projection and retraction operators to a subset $\mathcal{D}$ of rotations.
elements.sent-2411-079: The pseudocode for the general stochastic ReSync algorithm is outlined in Algorithm \ref{alg:stochastic .}
elements.sent-2411-080: Additionally, we can enhance the stability of stochastic algorithms by incorporating the random reshuffling (RR) trick \cite{safran2020good,li2023convergence .}
elements.sent-2411-081: It is also noteworthy that BSGD offers an intuitive method for solving orientation determination problems.
elements.sent-2411-082: Given that the objective function \eqref{formula: LUD features a double-sum structure and symmetry, in each iteration, we simply sample a subset of rotations and optimize the corresponding subproblem: \begin{equation}\label{formula: LUD-sub} \min_{R_i \in SO(3), i\in S} \sum_{i, j \in S} \|R_i c_{ij}-R_j c_{ji}\|_2.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}\label{formula: LUD-sub} \min_{R_i \in SO(3), i\in S} \sum_{i, j \in S} \|R_i c_{ij}-R_j c_{ji}\|_2.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-083: Another advantage of BSGD is its capacity for parallel execution.
elements.sent-2411-084: In each iteration, the rotations can be divided into separate blocks, allowing for the simultaneous optimization of independent subproblems.
elements.sent-2411-085: This underscores BSGD's computational benefits for large-scale applications.
elements.sent-2411-086: In contrast, such parallelization is not feasible with BCD, as it employs a Gauss-Seidel-style update that necessitates sequential execution.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Spectral norm constraints for low SNR
elements.figure-2411-001: \subfigure[Ground-truth]{\includegraphics[width=0.2\textwidth]{pics/R_orig_2d.pdf}} \subfigure[ReSync]{\includegraphics[width=0.2\textwidth]{pics/R_resync_2d.pdf}} \subfigure[ReSync-norm]{\includegraphics[width=0.23\textwidth]{pics/R_resync_norm_2d.pdf}} \caption{2D visualization of viewing directions by ground-truth and ReSync algorithms ($K=1000,SNR=1/64$). Each point represents a 3D viewing direction. The highly clustered area is in purple, while low-density area is in blue.} \label{fig:vis-direction}
elements.sent-2411-087: When the images are extremely noisy, there is an empirical observation that most algorithms fail to find high-accuracy solutions.
elements.sent-2411-088: The explanation for this observation is that, in this case, the solved viewing directions are highly clustered at some small region and thus it is far from uniformly distributed ground truth \cite{wang2013orientation .}
elements.sent-2411-089: We also provide visualization of projection direction exhibited in \Cref{fig:vis-direction (a) (b).}
elements.sent-2411-090: Denote by $\widetilde{R \in \mathbb{R ^{2K \times 2}$ the matrix consists of the first two rows of each $R_i, 1 \leq i \leq K$.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: As suggested in \cite{wang2013orientation , in this case, the Gram matrix $G=\widetilde{R \widetilde{R ^T$ has just two dominant eigenvalues, instead of three.}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Therefore, to prevent clustering, spectral norm constraint \(\|G\|_2 \preceq \alpha K\) is added to the original formulation \eqref{formula: LS and \eqref{formula: LUD where \(\alpha = [\frac{2 {3 ,1) \) is a hyperparameter.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Besides, if these rotations are uniformly sampled, then \(\alpha\) is close to \(\frac{2 {3}\).}
elements.sent-2411-094: However, SDR is always time-consuming, so we incorporate spectral norm constraints into our algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-095: We add a surrogate constraint \(\widetilde{R ^T \widetilde{R = \alpha K I_3\) to get the following optimization problem \begin{equation}\label{formula: LUD-norm} \begin{aligned} \min_{R_1, \ldots, R_K \in SO(3)} f(R) = \sum_{i \neq j} \|R_i c_{ij} - R_j c_{ji}\|_2 \text{subject to} \widetilde{R}^T \widetilde{R} = \alpha K I_3, \quad 1 \leq i \leq K. \end{aligned}\end{equation} We have adapted the Riemannian subgradient method by substituting the QR retraction with alternating projection onto two Riemannian manifolds: $R_1, \ldots, R_K \in SO(3)$ and $\widetilde{R ^T \widetilde{R = \alpha K I_3$.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}\label{formula: LUD-norm}  \min_{R_1, \ldots, R_K \in SO(3)} f(R) = \sum_{i \neq j} \|R_i c_{ij} - R_j c_{ji}\|_2 subject to \widetilde{R}^T \widetilde{R} = \alpha K I_3, \quad 1 \leq i \leq K. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-096: This adaptation, called ReSync-norm, demonstrates its effectiveness in preventing clustering viewing directions, as visualized in \Cref{fig:vis-direction (c).}
elements.sent-2411-097: In this section, we demonstrate the global convergence of our proposed Riemannian subgradient method, including its stochastic variants: stochastic Riemannian subgradient, block coordinate Riemannian subgradient, and block stochastic Riemannian subgradient methods.
elements.sent-2411-098: We establish that all these methods achieve an iteration complexity of $\mathcal{O (\varepsilon^{-4 )$ to reduce a chosen stationarity measure below $\varepsilon$.}}
elements.sent-2411-099: This complexity guarantee coincides with estimates provided in \cite{davis2019stochastic and \cite{li2021weakly for several algorithms designed to solve weakly convex minimization problems.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first complexity result for the block stochastic Riemannian subgradient method.
elements.sent-2411-101: Considering the influence of Stiefel manifold constraints on the problem, we adopt a stationarity measure that corresponds to the gradient of the Moreau envelope for weakly convex functions, as detailed in \cite{li2021weakly .}
elements.sent-2411-102: This choice of stationarity measure draws inspiration from recent research on weakly convex minimization in Euclidean spaces, as discussed in\cite{davis2019stochastic,drusvyatskiy2019efficiency .}
elements.sent-2411-103: First, let us define analogs of the Moreau envelope as \begin{equation}\label{eq:Moreau envelope} f_{\lambda}(X) = \min_{Y\in Stiefel(3K,3)} \left\{ f(Y) + \frac{1}{2\lambda} \|Y - X\|_F^2 \right\}, \quad X \in Stiefel(3K,3),\end{equation} and the proximal mapping for any \( \lambda > 0 \) for the problem \eqref{formula: LUD : \begin{equation}P_{\lambda f} (X) = \arg\min_{Y\in \text{Stiefel}(3K,3)} \left\{ f(Y) + \frac{1}{2\lambda} \|Y - X\|_F^2 \right\}, \quad X \in \text{Stiefel}(3K,3).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Moreau envelope} f_{\lambda}(X) = \min_{Y\in Stiefel(3K,3)} \left\{ f(Y) + \frac{1}{2\lambda} \|Y - X\|_F^2 \right\}, \quad X \in Stiefel(3K,3),\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}P_{\lambda f} (X) = \arg\min_{Y\in Stiefel(3K,3)} \left\{ f(Y) + \frac{1}{2\lambda} \|Y - X\|_F^2 \right\}, \quad X \in Stiefel(3K,3).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-104: We need to notice that, we call $X$ a stationary point of problem \eqref{formula: LUD if $X \in \text{Stiefel (3K,3)$ satisfies the following first-order optimality condition: \begin{equation}\label{eq:first order optimality} {0} \in \partial_{\mathbf{R}}f(X).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: According to the first-order optimality condition of $P_{\lambda f (X)$, \eqref{ineq:proxi-opt and \eqref{ineq:dist-theta show that \begin{equation}\label{eq:surrogate optimality} \text{dist} \left( 0,\partial_{\mathcal{R}} f \left(P_{\lambda f} (X)\right) \right) \leq \lambda^{-1} \cdot \left\| P_{\lambda f} (X) - X \right\|_F =: \Theta(X).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}\label{eq:surrogate optimality} dist \left( 0,\partial_{R} f \left(P_{\lambda f} (X)\right) \right) \leq \lambda^{-1} \cdot \left\| P_{\lambda f} (X) - X \right\|_F =: \Theta(X).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-106: Specifically, from \eqref{eq:surrogate optimality , we observe that when $\Theta(X) = 0$, we have $P_{\lambda f (X) = X$ and thus $ \text{dist \left( 0,\partial_{\mathcal{R f \left(X\right) \right) = 0$, which implies $X$ is a stationary point of problem \eqref{formula: LUD .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: This motivates us to use $X \mapsto \Theta(X)$ as a stationarity measure for problem \eqref{formula: LUD , and we call $X\in\text{Stiefel (3K,3)$ an $\varepsilon$-approximate stationary point of problem \eqref{formula: LUD if it satisfies $\Theta(X)\leq \varepsilon$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: Since the Riemannian subgradient, the stochastic Riemannian subgradient, and the block coordinate Riemannian subgradient methods are all special cases of the block stochastic Riemannian subgradient method.
elements.sent-2411-109: So it is enough to estimate the iteration complexity of Algorithm \ref{alg:stochastic .}
elements.sent-2411-110: Assuming a constant step size, \(\mu_t = \frac{1 {\sqrt{T+1 }\), \((k=0,1,\ldots)\), where \(T\) represents the total number of iterations, Algorithm \ref{alg:stochastic uniformly and randomly selects an index \(\overline{t}\) from \(\{1,\ldots,T\}\) and returns the corresponding \(X_{\overline{t }\).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Then, we have \begin{equation}E\left[ \Theta^2\left(X_{\overline{t}}\right) \right] \leq \frac{c_1\big(f_{\lambda}(X_0) - \min f_{\lambda}\big) + c_2}{\sqrt{T+1}},\end{equation} where the constants $c_1$ and $c_2$ are given in \eqref{eq:c1c2 .}
elements.sent-2411-112: In particular, the iteration complexity of Algorithm \ref{alg:stochastic for computing an $\varepsilon$-nearly stationary point of the problem \eqref{formula: LUD is $O(\varepsilon^{-4 )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: The proof can be found in Appendix \ref{app:conv-analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-114: To evaluate the performance of the ReSync algorithm and its stochastic variants, we conduct experiments on both simulated rotations and noisy projections of particles.
elements.sent-2411-115: For benchmark methods, we choose eigenvector relaxation \cite{singer2011three , PGD method for LS and LUD formulations \cite{pan2023orientation , as well as IRLS method \cite{wang2013orientation .}}}
elements.sent-2411-116: All experiments are executed on a machine with AMD Ryzen 9 7950X CPU with 16 cores, running at 4.50 GHz.
elements.sent-2411-117: In addition, all results are averaged over 10 independent trials.
elements.sent-2411-119: In line with prior studies, we use mean-squared error (MSE) as the metric to assess the accuracy of estimated orientations.
elements.sent-2411-120: The MSE, calculated between estimated rotations \(\hat{R _1,..., \hat{R _K\) and the underlying ground-truth rotations \(R_1,..., R_K\), is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:MSE} MSE = \min_{O \in SO(3)} \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^K \|R_i - O \hat{R}_i\|_F^2.\end{equation}.}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \subfigure{\includegraphics[width=0.25\textwidth]{pics/benchmark_P_0.1K_3000.pdf}} \subfigure{\includegraphics[width=0.25\textwidth]{pics/benchmark_P_0.3K_3000.pdf}} \subfigure{\includegraphics[width=0.26\textwidth]{pics/benchmark_P_0.5K_3000.pdf}} \caption{Comparison with other orientation determination algorithms under synthetic data ($K=3000$). From left to right, the detection rate are $p=0.1, 0.3, 0.5$, respectively.} \label{fig:plot_benchmark}
elements.figure-2411-003: \subfigure{\includegraphics[width=0.25\textwidth]{pics/P_0.1K_3000.pdf}} \subfigure{\includegraphics[width=0.25\textwidth]{pics/P_0.3K_1000.pdf}} \subfigure{\includegraphics[width=0.26\textwidth]{pics/P_0.5K_3000.pdf}} \caption{Convergence of ReSync-BSGD under synthetic data ($K=3000$). From left to right, the detection rate are $p=0.1, 0.3, 0.5$, respectively.} \label{fig:plot_random}
elements.sent-2411-121: To assess the efficiency of different algorithms, we conduct experiments using simulated rotations.
elements.sent-2411-122: First, we generate \(K\) ground-truth rotations uniformly from \(\text{SO (3)\) and calculate pairwise common-line indexes with angular resolution \(n_{\theta =360\).}}
elements.sent-2411-123: To simulate the corrupted nature of Cryo-EM images, we introduce a detection rate \(p\), referring to the probability that common lines are correctly detected.
elements.sent-2411-124: Specifically, each common-line pair \((c_{ij ,c_{ji )\) remains unchanged with probability \(p\), or is replaced by a uniform random pair with probability \(1-p\).}}
elements.sent-2411-126: Notably, simulations at \(p=0.05\) or \(K=5000\) are rarely seen in previous studies.. Convergence verification.
elements.sent-2411-127: In \Cref{fig:plot_benchmark , we illustrate the convergence behavior of ReSync algorithms alongside other benchmarks for \(K=3000\) under detection rates \(p=0.1, 0.3, 0.5\).}
elements.sent-2411-128: As the figure shows, ReSync algorithm converges rapidly to a high-accuracy solution, particularly at lower $p$ values.
elements.sent-2411-129: In \Cref{fig:plot_random , we display the convergence behavior of ReSync-BSGD with varying filter ratios $\rho$ under identical settings.}
elements.sent-2411-130: The figure illustrates a consistent trade-off between efficiency and accuracy: as $\rho$ decreases, the convergence rate improves at the expense of accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-131: Additionally, as the detection rate increases, the accuracy differences among various $\rho$ values become less pronounced.. Efficiency benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-132: The MSE and running times for various methods with \(K=3000\) and \(K = 5000\) are listed in \Cref{tab:simu .}
elements.sent-2411-133: For consistent comparison, we set the same stopping precision for all methods, achievable within the maximum iteration count for most methods.
elements.sent-2411-134: Every method is warm-started with the solution from eigenvector relaxation.
elements.sent-2411-135: As shown in \Cref{tab:simu , ReSync-BSGD outperforms other methods in most scenarios by achieving comparable accuracy and a 5 to 30-fold speedup compared to state-of-the-art methods.}

elements.figure-2411-005: \subfigure[clean]{\includegraphics[width=0.14\textwidth]{pics/clean.png}} \subfigure[SNR=1/16]{\includegraphics[width=0.14\textwidth]{pics/snr16.png}} \subfigure[SNR=1/32]{\includegraphics[width=0.14\textwidth]{pics/snr32.png}} \subfigure[SNR=1/64]{\includegraphics[width=0.14\textwidth]{pics/snr64.png}} \subfigure[SNR=1/128]{\includegraphics[width=0.14\textwidth]{pics/snr128.png}} \caption{Projection images of 50-S ribosomal subunit under different SNR levels.} \label{fig:SNR}
elements.sent-2411-136: To test the real-world applicability of different methods, we conduct experiments using real projection data.
elements.sent-2411-137: In line with prior studies \cite{wang2013orientation,pan2023orientation , we select 50-S ribosomal subunit with volume \(65\times 65 \times 65\) as the observing target, whose 3D density map is available.}
elements.sent-2411-138: We start by sampling ground-truth rotations \(R_1,..., R_K\), then \(K\) clean projection images with pixel size \(129\times 129\) are generated from the underlying density map.
elements.sent-2411-139: To simulate noisy Cryo-EM images, we add Gaussian noise to these images according to certain SNR.
elements.sent-2411-140: Subsequently, common-lines are estimated from these noisy projections by conducting Fourier transform (angular resolution \(n_{\theta =360\), radial resolution \(n_r=100\)) and finding the most similar pair of Fourier coefficients.}
elements.sent-2411-141: Considering that Cryo-EM images feature low SNR and large numbers, we set \(\text{SNR =(1/16, 1/32, 1/64, 1/128)\) and \(K=(3000,5000)\).}
elements.sent-2411-142: To the best of our knowledge, this marks the first valid documentation of experimental results for \(\text{SNR =1/128\) in the literature.}
elements.sent-2411-143: Additionally, we present projection images of the 50-S ribosomal subunit under this SNR setting in \Cref{fig:SNR .}
elements.sent-2411-144: Notably, at an \(\text{SNR}\) of 1/128, the images are predominantly obscured by significant noise.. Efficiency benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-145: The experiment results for \(K=3000\) and \(K = 5000\) are shown in \Cref{tab:real .}
elements.sent-2411-146: The stopping criteria are triggered when the relative distance of adjacent iterates is smaller than \(10^{-5}\).
elements.sent-2411-147: All methods are warm-started by eigenvector relaxations.
elements.sent-2411-148: As these tables show, when SNR is \(1/16\) or \(1/32\), most methods converge to a high-accuracy solution, and ReSync-BSGD achieves 15-100 times acceleration compared with LUD-PGD and LUD-IRLS.
elements.sent-2411-149: When \(\text{SNR =1/64\) and \(K=3000\), all benchmark methods diverge in precision but our stochastic ReSync algorithms can prevent divergence to some extent.}
elements.sent-2411-150: However, if SNR decreases to \(1/128\), eigenvector relaxation fails to produce high-quality initialization points and all methods perform poorly in accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-151: Previous results reveal that when noise levels are high (with $p=0.05$ or $\text{SNR =1/64, 1/128$), most methods get stuck at solutions of low accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-152: As discussed in \Cref{sec:norm , this could be addressed by applying a spectral norm constraint and image denoising techniques to enhance performance.}
elements.sent-2411-153: To assess the efficacy of norm-constrained methods (i.e., SDR-ADMM and ReSync-norm), we conducted experiments on real data, setting the projection number at $K=3000$ across various SNR levels.
elements.sent-2411-154: We chose a hyperparameter \(\alpha = \frac{2 {3}\), based on the prior knowledge that viewing directions are uniformly distributed in large samples.}
elements.sent-2411-155: The results, detailed in \Cref{tab:norm , indicate that both SDR-ADMM and ReSync-norm perform well at lower SNRs, with SNR\(=1/64\), yielding relatively low MSEs.}
elements.sent-2411-156: Notably, ReSync-norm maintains accurate and stable performance as SNR ranges from $1/16$ to $1/64$, whereas SDR-ADMM struggles with higher SNRs.
elements.sent-2411-157: Additionally, according to \Cref{tab:norm-time , ReSync-norm significantly outperforms SDR-ADMM in computational efficiency, achieving a 25 to 60-fold speed increase.}
elements.sent-2411-158: While norm-constrained methods like SDR-ADMM and ReSync-norm demonstrate superior performance when SNR\(\geq 1/64\), their effectiveness diminishes when faced with extremely high noise levels, such as SNR\(= 1/128\).
elements.sent-2411-159: To mitigate the reduced effectiveness of norm-constrained methods at extremely high noise levels, we have explored the application of image-denoising techniques.
elements.sent-2411-160: Class averaging, the most prevalent preprocessing technique for Cryo-EM images as outlined by \cite{frank2006three , clusters images with similar viewing angles and averages them to enhance signal clarity.}
elements.sent-2411-161: For this clustering, we utilize Vector Diffusion Maps (VDM) \cite{singer2012vector , which effectively organize complex data in low-dimensional spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-162: The results of these experiments, displayed in the last column of \Cref{tab:norm , indicate that most methods achieve higher precision, likely due to the improved quality of initial data.}
elements.figure-2411-007: \caption{MSE for different methods under various SNR levels and denoised images. Here $K=3000$, $\alpha=2/3$, and methods with spectral norm constraint are in bold font.} \label{tab:norm} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.0} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{8pt} 0.8\textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{ccccccc} SNR 1/16 1/32 1/64 1/128 1/128, denoised Eig 0.0328 0.1016 0.5962 2.7131 0.8241 LS-PGD 0.1001 0.4595 1.2104 2.2516 0.7518 LUD-PGD 0.0068 0.0626 2.0459 2.1717 4.9269 LUD-IRLS-PGD 0.0071 0.0904 2.0467 2.2004 4.3826 SDR-ADMM 0.0315 0.1283 0.3634 2.1946 0.7249 ReSync 0.0069 0.0599 0.9577 2.1768 0.7289 ReSync-norm 0.0062 0.0180 0.2495 2.3273 0.7282 \end{tabular} }

Here $K=3000$, $\alpha=2/3$, and methods with spectral norm constraint are in bold font.} \label{tab:norm-time} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.0} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{8pt} 0.8\textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{cccccc} SNR 1/16 1/32 1/64 1/128, denoised Eig 17.40 17.12 17.02 17.10 LS-PGD 32.59 143.96 201.93 200.73 LUD-PGD 45.60 75.98 203.49 212.24 LUD-IRLS-PGD 153.56 471.80 820.58 772.56 SDR-ADMM 13957.06 14883.00 13722.88 9377.16 ReSync 23.70 25.84 27.58 30.16 ReSync-norm 232.77 296.59 359.38 406.14 \end{tabular} }
elements.sent-2411-163: In this paper, we tackle the orientation determination problem in Cryo-EM images.
elements.sent-2411-164: We adopt a common-line-based LUD formulation and propose to use the Riemannian subgradient method to solve this nonconvex and nonsmooth synchronization problem.
elements.sent-2411-165: For tackling a large number of images, we propose a block stochastic Riemannian subgradient method, which achieves a surprising acceleration effect and surpasses its SGD and BCD counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-166: To handle the noisy images, we incorporate spectral norm constraint into our algorithm and apply an alternating projection method to guarantee feasibility.
elements.sent-2411-167: Experiments on both synthetic and real data underscore the superiority in effectiveness and efficiency of our proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-168: We will discuss the limitations from both empirical and theoretical aspects.
elements.sent-2411-169: In our experiments on real data, we have established the effectiveness of the ReSync-norm algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-170: However, the alternative projection in each iteration is costly in computation.
elements.sent-2411-171: The remaining issue is to develop a more efficient algorithm for tackling norm constraints.
elements.sent-2411-172: Theoretically, we have established the convergence results of ReSync-BSGD, but an in-depth analysis of the convergence rate, especially its superiority over SGD and BCD, is not involved in our result.
elements.sent-2411-173: As a consequence, another research direction is to explore the property of the BSGD algorithm, especially out of the scope of some specific synchronization problem.. \bibliographystyle{plainnat .}
elements.sent-2411-174: We will follow the proof methodology outlined in \cite{li2021weakly , starting with the introduction of the weak convexity inequality under Riemannian manifold constraints as an essential tool.}
elements.sent-2411-175: It is worth mentioning that the introduction of the weak convexity inequality under Riemannian manifold constraints, which is used for the restrictions on weakly convex functions on the Stiefel manifold, is a fundamental component in the proof of convergence results in it.

elements.formula-2411-019: \begin{equation}\label{ineq:proxi-opt} 0 \in \partial_{R} f \left(P_{\lambda f} (X)\right) + \frac{1}{\lambda} P_{T_{P_{\lambda f} (X)}St} \left( P_{\lambda f} (X) - X\right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-178: Therefore, we have \begin{equation}\label{ineq:dist-theta} \begin{split} \text{dist} \left( 0,\partial_{\mathcal{R}} f \left(P_{\lambda f} (X)\right) \right) \leq \lambda^{-1}\cdot \left\| \mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{T}_{P_{\lambda f} (X)}\text{St}} \left( P_{\lambda f} (X) - X\right) \right\|_F \leq \lambda^{-1} \cdot \left\| P_{\lambda f} (X) - X \right\|_F =: \Theta(X). \end{split}\end{equation}.

elements.subsection-2411-007: Construction of the Riemannian Block Stochastic Subgradient Oracle
elements.sent-2411-179: With the tools ready, now we can start the algorithm's convergence analysis.
elements.sent-2411-180: Considering the block stochastic gradient descent (BSGD), the randomness comes from the random rearrangement of \(\{1,2,\cdots,K\}\) before each iteration, which leads to the random blocking of \(\{[1], \cdots, [\rho K]\}\).
elements.sent-2411-181: This affects the computation of the approximate subgradient and the update of variables for each block.
elements.sent-2411-182: It's important to note that we can choose \(\mathcal{S = \mathcal{D}\), and considering the properties of the subgradient computation, we can immediately conclude that the computation of the approximate subgradient and the update of variables within each block are independent and self-contained.}
elements.sent-2411-183: Let the sample space formed by the random partitioning of \(\{1,2,\cdots,K\}\) be denoted as\ \(\Omega \in \mathbf{R ^K\).}
elements.sent-2411-184: Then, \(\zeta_t\) represents the outcome of the \(t\)-th random partitioning, which is distributed uniformly over \(\Omega\).
elements.sent-2411-185: We can denoted it as \(\zeta_t \sim U(\Omega)\).
elements.sent-2411-186: Now, assume that the Riemannian random reshuffling block subgradient method is equipped with a Riemannian Block Stochastic Subgradient Oracle, which possesses the following properties: (a) The oracle can generate independent and identically distributed samples according to the distribution \(U(\Omega)\).
elements.sent-2411-187: In this setup, the random reshuffling of \(\{1, 2, \cdots, K\}\) results in blocks \([1], [2], \cdots, [m]\) with\ $m=\rho K$ and each block having a size of \(1/\rho\).
elements.sent-2411-188: For any \( i \in [n] \), \begin{equation}g_i(X_i,\zeta)=\sum_{\substack{j \in [n] j \neq i}} f_{i, j}(X_i)\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-189: Next, we will establish the relationship between the alternative stationary measure \(\Theta\) and the Moreau envelope \(f_\lambda\) in terms of sufficient reduction under expectation.
elements.sent-2411-190: Given \(X \in \text{Stiefel (3K,3)\) and \(\xi \in T_X St\), consider a retraction based on QR decomposition, then it satisfies \begin{equation}\left\|Retr_{X}(\xi) - \overline X \right\|_F \le \|X + \xi - \overline X\|_F + b \|\xi\|_F^2,\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-191: The retraction on the compact submanifold \(\text{Stiefel (3K,3)\) satisfies the second-order boundedness property.}
elements.sent-2411-192: So there exists a constant \(b \ge 0\) such that for all \(X \in \text{Stiefel (3K,3)\) and \(\xi \in T_X St\), \begin{equation}\| Retr_{X}(\xi) - X - \xi \|_F \le b \|\xi\|_F^2.\end{equation}. Then, \begin{equation}\left\|Retr_{X}(\xi) - \overline X \right\|_F = \| (X + \xi) - \overline X + Retr_{X}(\xi) - (X+\xi) \|_F \le \|X + \xi - \overline X\|_F + b \|\xi\|_F^2,\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Let \({X_t}\) be a sequence generated by the Riemannian block stochastic subgradient method (BSGD), with arbitrary initialization.

elements.sent-2411-200: The last inequality again utilizes \(\left\|\widetilde\nabla_{R g_i(X_i, \zeta)\right\| \leq \left\|\widetilde\nabla g_i(X_i, \zeta)\right\| \leq B\).}
elements.sent-2411-201: According to \Cref{thm:Riemannian subgradient inequality , it holds that \begin{equation}E_{\zeta_t\sim U(\Omega) }\left[f_\lambda(X^{t+1})\right] \le f\left(P_{\lambda f}(X^t)\right)+\frac{2\mu_tc}{\lambda}\left[f\left(P_{\lambda f}(X^t),\zeta_t\right)-f\left(X^t,\zeta_t\right)\right] \quad+\frac{(L+\tau)\mu_t}{\lambda}\|P_{\lambda f}(X^t)-X^t\|_F^2 +\frac{KM^2}{\lambda}\mu_t^4+\frac{KB^2}{\lambda}\mu_t^2\end{equation} where \(c\) is a constant.}
elements.sent-2411-202: According to the definitions of the Moreau envelope and the proximal mapping, the following holds: \begin{equation}f\left(P_{\lambda f}(X_t)\right)-f(X_t)+\frac{L+\tau}{2c}\|P_{\lambda f}(X_t)-X_t\|_F^2 =-\left[f(X_t)-f\left(P_{\lambda f}(X_t)\right)-\frac{L+\tau}{2c}\|P_{\lambda f}(X_t)-X_t\|_F^2\right] =-\left[f(X_t)-\left(f\left(P_{\lambda f}(X_t)\right)+\frac{1}{2\lambda}\|P_{\lambda f}(X_t)-X_t\|_F^2\right)+\left(\frac{1}{2\lambda}-\frac{L+\tau}{2c}\right)\|P_{\lambda f}(X_t)-X_t\|_F^2\right] \le -\left(\frac{1}{2\lambda}-\frac{L+\tau}{2c}\right)\|P_{\lambda f}(X_t)-X_t\|_F^2\end{equation} Thus, it can be deduced \begin{equation}E_{\zeta_t\sim U(\Omega) }\left[f_\lambda(X_{t+1})\right] \le f_{\lambda} (X_{t})-\frac{2\mu_t c}{\lambda}\left(\frac{1}{2\lambda}-\frac{L+\tau}{2c}\right)\|P_{\lambda f}(X_t)-X_t\|_F^2+\frac{KM^2}{\lambda}\mu_t^4+\frac{KB^2}{\lambda}\mu_t^2\end{equation} After taking the expectation on both sides of the equation with respect to all previous realizations \( \left(\zeta_0, \cdots, \zeta_{t-1}\right)\), we obtain \begin{equation}E\left[f_\lambda(X_{t+1})\right] \le E\left[f_{\lambda} (X_{t})\right]-\frac{2\mu_t c}{\lambda}\left(\frac{1}{2\lambda}-\frac{L+\tau}{2c}\right)E\left[\|P_{\lambda f}(X_t)-X_t\|_F^2\right]+\frac{KM^2}{\lambda}\mu_t^4+\frac{KB^2}{\lambda}\mu_t^2\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-203: Using \Cref{eq:proposition6.3 , we can prove Theorem \ref{thm:convergence and obtain our iteration complexity result for the block stochastic gradient descent (BSGD).}}
elements.sent-2411-204: To complete the proof, the remaining task is to substitute \(\mu_t = \frac{1 {\sqrt{T+1 }\) into the above inequality and choose \begin{equation}\label{eq:c1c2} c_1 = \frac{1}{c\lambda \left(\frac{c}{\lambda} - \frac{L+\tau}{c}\right)} \quad and \quad c_2 = \frac{K(M^2+B^2)}{\lambda}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-205: Note that the left-hand side is exactly \(E\left[\Theta^2\left(X_{\overline{t }\right)\right]\), where the expectation is taken over \(\zeta_0, \cdots, \zeta_{T-1 , \overline{t}\).}}
